## Findings

### F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-001 — Volatility3 windows.netscan output contains a TCPv4 row in LIST… *(confidence: HIGH)*

Volatility3 windows.netscan output contains a TCPv4 row in LISTENING state bound to local address 0.0.0.0 port 3262 with PID 12528 and Owner subject_srv.ex, Created 2018-09-06 19:37:07.000000 UTC (with a matching TCPv6 :: 3262 LISTENING row for the same PID).. The process subject_srv.ex (PID 12528) is bound as a server socket on TCP port 3262 across all interfaces (0.0.0.0 and ::). Port 3262 is a non-standard high port not associated with any legitimate built-in Windows service, and the process name does not match any known Windows/OEM binary. This is consistent with an attacker-installed network listener / backdoor awaiting inbound connections..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img#netscan offset 0x8702b043d6a0; PID 12528; TCP 0.0.0.0:3262 LISTENING` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -q -f /tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `3262`, `LISTENING`, `12528`, `subject_srv.ex`, `0.0.0.0`, `2018-09-06 19:37:07.000000 UTC` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 a1ac809a90dc6188…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-001`.
- **Council Receipt:** `f25fe083954a3d2e…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-002 — Volatility3 windows.psscan output lists a process named subject… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

Volatility3 windows.psscan output lists a process named subject_srv.ex with PID 12528 and PPID 740, Wow64 = True, CreateTime 2018-09-06 19:37:05.000000 UTC and ExitTime N/A. The same psscan output lists PID 740 as services.exe.. subject_srv.ex (PID 12528) was launched by parent PID 740, which the same output identifies as services.exe (the Windows Service Control Manager) — i.e. subject_srv.ex is running as a Windows service. It is a 32-bit process (Wow64 = True) with a non-standard name not matching any known Windows/OEM binary, created 2018-09-06 19:37:05 UTC and still running at acquisition (ExitTime N/A). A newly created, oddly-named service binary is a common persistence mechanism. This is the same PID 12528 that owns the TCP 3262 listener verified in F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-001..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img#psscan PID 12528 (subject_srv.ex), PPID 740 (services.exe), Offset(V) 0x8702b02795c0` — tool `volatility3 windows.psscan`, command `vol -q -f /tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img windows.psscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `12528`, `740`, `subject_srv.ex`, `services.exe`, `True`, `2018-09-06 19:37:05.000000 UTC` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 ccc300ded144b5e2…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-002`.
- **Council Receipt:** `8955c9bf834478e4…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-003 — Volatility3 windows.netscan output contains an ESTABLISHED TCPv… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

Volatility3 windows.netscan output contains an ESTABLISHED TCPv4 row with local endpoint 172.16.7.11:3262 and foreign endpoint 172.16.5.50:56722 (PID/Owner fields shown as '-'). The same netscan output also contains a LISTENING TCPv4 row on 0.0.0.0:3262 owned by PID 12528 subject_srv.ex.. There is an active (ESTABLISHED) TCP session whose local endpoint is 172.16.7.11 port 3262 — the same port on which subject_srv.ex (PID 12528) is LISTENING in the same output — with internal peer 172.16.5.50:56722. This indicates an inbound session from host 172.16.5.50 to the subject_srv.ex listener on the workstation (172.16.7.11) at acquisition time. The connection record does not bind a PID (Owner '-'), so attribution to subject_srv.ex is by local-port correlation rather than direct process ownership; 172.16.5.50 is the apparent remote peer of the backdoor..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img#netscan offset 0x8702b325f810; TCP 172.16.7.11:3262 <-> 172.16.5.50:56722 ESTABLISHED` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -q -f /tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `172.16.7.11`, `3262`, `172.16.5.50`, `56722`, `ESTABLISHED`, `LISTENING`, `subject_srv.ex`, `12528` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 4c3f5b487f37a479…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-003`.
- **Council Receipt:** `43e124b9d74bee4f…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

### F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-004 — Volatility3 windows.netscan output shows multiple TCPv4 rows wh… *(confidence: MEDIUM)*

Volatility3 windows.netscan output shows multiple TCPv4 rows whose local address is 172.16.7.11 connecting outbound to several distinct internal hosts/ports: an ESTABLISHED session 172.16.7.11:56150 -> 172.16.5.21:5985, plus CLOSED rows to 172.16.5.21:5985, 172.16.4.10:8080 (repeated), 172.16.4.4:135, 172.16.5.20:443, and 172.16.5.25:5682. All of these rows show PID and Owner as '-'.. The workstation (local IP 172.16.7.11) initiated outbound TCP connections to multiple distinct internal hosts and service ports, including an ESTABLISHED connection to 172.16.5.21 on port 5985 (WS-Management / WinRM, used for remote command execution) and repeated connections to 172.16.4.10 on 8080, plus 172.16.4.4:135 (RPC/DCOM), 172.16.5.20:443 and 172.16.5.25:5682. The fan-out to several internal hosts and the use of remote-management port 5985 are consistent with internal reconnaissance and lateral movement originating from this workstation. Because every one of these connection records shows Owner '-', netscan does not attribute them to a specific process; the responsible process cannot be established from this output alone..

- **Evidence pointer:** `/tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img#netscan rows for LocalAddr 172.16.7.11 -> 172.16.5.21:5985 (ESTABLISHED, offset 0x8702b198f870) and CLOSED rows to 172.16.4.10:8080, 172.16.4.4:135, 172.16.5.20:443, 172.16.5.25:5682` — tool `volatility3 windows.netscan`, command `vol -q -f /tmp/wkstn01/base-wkstn-01-memory.img windows.netscan`
- **Cited evidence:** `172.16.7.11`, `172.16.5.21`, `5985`, `ESTABLISHED`, `172.16.4.10`, `8080`, `172.16.4.4`, `135`, `172.16.5.20`, `443`, `172.16.5.25`, `5682`, `CLOSED` — every cited token resolves in the real tool output (Citation seat).
- **Integrity:** `output_sha256 69f815cd9ccbfdde…` — re-hash verified; **independently reproducible** with `csift trace --rerun F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-004`.
- **Council Receipt:** `bbcc90a155e1ef14…` — seats: citation=SUPPORTED, tool-semantics=SUPPORTED, contradiction=SUPPORTED, inference=SUPPORTED

## Confidence & limitations

- Confidence is stated per finding (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / SPECULATIVE) and reflects what a single artifact
  can bear; nothing here is asserted beyond the cited evidence.
- "Observation" is the literal tool output; "interpretation" is the analyst's inference. The Council's
  Citation, Tool-semantics, Contradiction, and Inference seats independently checked the leap between them.
- Every finding traces to its tool execution (`csift trace F-analyst-SRL18-WKSTN-MEM-001`) and can be
  re-executed against the evidence (`csift trace --rerun …`); the Council Receipts are hash-chained.
- This narrative is generated from the audit substrate, not hand-written — it cannot contain a claim that
  is not a verified node in the graph.
